headline: Wheat Dumps 14¢, Milk Rockets 15% Higher
subheadline: Strong dollar pressures grain exports while dairy rallies hard on tight supplies.
teaser: Wheat bleeds on export pressure. Milk surges. Illinois gets soaked.

one_number.why: That's a 5-day total with most falling Wednesday-Thursday. Plan on field delays through next weekend.

field_call.label: SPRAY WINDOW
field_call.detail: Iowa and Nebraska have a dry 3-day window starting now. Illinois and Wisconsin close Wednesday.

sections.title: Grains & Oilseeds
bullets: Corn $4.45¼, down 2¾¢. Dec '26 at $4.69¼. The 24¢ carry looks healthy but watch basis tighten as farmers hold.
Beans $11.61¾, down 8½¢. Meal at $312.50, oil at 62.68¢. Crush margins squeezed by weaker meal — livestock feeding slowing seasonally.
Wheat $5.76¼, down 14½¢ in the worst session in weeks. Dollar strength kills export competitiveness against Black Sea supplies.
January crush: 228 million bushels processed, 505 million bushels of corn to ethanol. Both in line with expectations.

sections.title: Livestock & Dairy
bullets: Live cattle $233.65, up $1.42. Feeders $353.70, up $2.73. Cash trade this week should open firm on tight supplies and steady beef demand.
Hogs $95.58, down a nickel. Essentially flat as spring farrowing intentions keep prices range-bound.
Class III milk $17.21, up $2.29 — a 15% surge. Tight milk production and strong cheese demand supporting dairy. Best rally in months.

sections.title: Weather & Fieldwork
bullets: Central Illinois gets 1.87 inches through Friday, with 1.28 inches Wednesday alone. Burndown and early prep work stops mid-week.
Iowa and Nebraska stay mostly dry through Wednesday — 0.34 inches and 0.16 inches 5-day totals. Get anhydrous down if you're ready.
Temps: 40s-50s today, warming to 60s-70s by Friday across most of the Belt. Soil temps still cool but trending right for early April work.
Bottom line: Use the next 48 hours in Iowa, Nebraska, and South Dakota. Illinois is done by Wednesday.

sections.title: Energy & Macro
bullets: Crude $71.03, down 28¢. Diesel costs holding steady but watch fertilizer — urea jumped $60-$80/ton this week on Strait of Hormuz supply fears.
Dollar at 98.51, up 66 basis points. That's the grain export killer today — makes U.S. corn and beans more expensive for foreign buyers.
Natural gas up 9¢ to $2.98. Anhydrous plants watching closely but nitrogen prices already spiking on Middle East tensions, not natgas.

sections.title: Policy & Trade
bullets: USDA crop insurance: Revenue protection guarantees higher for beans, lower for corn this year per South Dakota agents. Lock in coverage before mid-March.
E15 expansion could boost corn demand but cut bean prices according to Missouri FAPRI report. More ethanol = less soybean meal acres.
U.S.-China ag trade still "complicated" per USDA's Foreign Ag Service. Over a billion consumers can't be ignored but competition from Brazil intensifies.

watch_list.label: Today
watch_list.item: Basis levels at your local elevator — farmer selling could tighten spreads fast.

watch_list.label: This Week
watch_list.item: Iran-Hormuz situation. Fertilizer prices could spike further if shipping stays disrupted.

watch_list.label: Horizon
watch_list.item: March 31 USDA Prospective Planting Report. Market's guessing corn acres down, bean acres up.

watch_list.label: Risk
watch_list.item: Strong dollar guts export demand just as South America wraps harvest. Basis could weaken fast.

watch_list.label: Opportunity
watch_list.item: Lock new-crop bean sales on any bounce over $11.75. Carry structure rewards storage but export risk is real.

the_more_you_know.title: Soybeans Crushed at Record Pace
the_more_you_know.body: U.S. crushers processed 75.2 million tons of soybeans in 2025 — a record. January alone saw 6.84 million tons (228 million bushels) go through the plants. That's strong domestic demand even as export pace lags.

daily_quote.text: The farmer has to be an optimist or he wouldn't still be a farmer.

source_summary: Market data from CME Group. Weather from multiple Corn Belt stations. USDA reports and ag news aggregated from DTN, Farm Progress, Brownfield, and regional sources.
